But the first thing I do when I sit down next to a woman is as quickly as possible. I try to let them know that I'm married. ## [15:08 - 18:09]

but quite often most of the things that I teach adults, I teach in some form or another with kids. And I actually do a lot of this work with children, 10 year olds, who I teach fifth graders. I teach them the same thing, which is when you're sitting next to someone at a lunch table or you're standing in line next to someone waiting to enter a library or you're sitting on a bus and you're going to a field trip and you happen to be seated with someone who you don't know very well or someone you've never really had a conversation with because there's a whole bunch of kids in your grade and you just don't know this kid. You have a chance to either remain silent and stare at your stupid phone quite literally and probably feel that worse about yourself when you're done or you have an opportunity to chat with people. So I teach kids. I don't call it the subtle art of storytelling with my kids. I call it small talk. I teach them how to engage in small talk, which is essentially asking questions and recognizing that when you are asked a question in return or when you're initially asked a question that you have a choice every single time. And it is a simple answer. Good, yes, great, sad, or it's a chance to share something of you. And just like I do in the webinar, I teach kids what people want to hear about you. And what they don't want to hear about you. And I'm pretty aggressive on the don't because the problem with kids is they often talk about things. Nobody wants to hear the rule I gave today. One of the rules I give to my students today was nobody ever wants to hear about you almost getting hurt. Almost getting hurt is boring. Now, if you almost broke your leg, meaning like the bone stuck out, but it happened not to be broken. Now we have a story. Now we have something interesting. ## [21:13 - 24:16]

like cooking food, unless they ask for it. Now, if they say, wow, that looks great. Can you tell me how to make it? Now you're all in. But I think a lot of times adults get wrapped up in processes that tend not to be terribly interesting or engaging mostly because they're not about you. Right. People want to hear about the person who's speaking, not the thing that was sort of disconnected from that person. Another terrible thing is stories about dealing with traffic traveling to a place. You know, someone went to New York City over the weekend and they want to tell me about the brutal drive down 95 and the three accidents and how the FDR was backed up and they couldn't believe getting over the Brooklyn Bridge could be so hard. And again, that's not a story about them. That is a process that is getting from one place to another. Unless I say, hey, you went into the city this weekend, I'm going next week. And can you tell me how the drive was? I want to be prepared for it. Unless you get a question like that, I think those are the kinds of things adults are fairly uninterested in. And yet quite often because we were sort of consumed in that process, we often think there's value in it because we spent an hour cooking dinner and it came out so great. We just assume everyone's going to want to hear it. I actually think most food stories are terrible because we can't taste the food. So as much as you want to tell us how great the food is, we can't actually taste it. So it's almost like an annoying story because we don't get the we don't get the conclusion. It's actually why I hate all cooking shows. I actually cook quite a bit now, but I don't like cooking shows because we're the cooking competition shows. ## [24:16 - 27:19]

Now, that's a story about me, right? That's about me and my son and baseball and things like that. The bad version, the bad response to that would be you telling me a baseball story. Because that's essentially, I tell you a baseball story and you're matching content for content. And oftentimes that feels like you're one upping me. It sounds like you're bragging. Sounds like you're looking for a story that's a little better than my Charlie baseball story. Instead, what you want to say to make people interested is you want to express empathy and we express empathy by matching theme, meaning and message rather than content. So what you hear from that or what I might hear from that is, oh, that's a story about how the joy of your childhood might not be an acceptable joy today. Or the world has changed so much that the things you loved as a kid are no longer lovable in today's world. And if you were able to tell a story about that, if you were able to say, oh, here's something in my life that used to be good. And now it's not like if you would use to play, it was that game where you threw the charts in the air, they were the things that would stab you and they had to they're like a legal all over the country. If you said like, oh, I used to love playing charts, but, you know, it turns out they're not they're not 20, 20, they're 1980. Suddenly you're connecting on theme, meaning a message. We're matching thoughts and beliefs and not content. I think that's what adults want. I think that's what all human beings want is they want to know that being heard not on a surface level, but an under the surface level, a below the surface level. If you told me a story about a time when your child taught you a lesson, right? Unrelated to baseball, but Charlie kind of taught me a lesson. And you were like, I remember the day my son taught me and you told me that story. That tells me you're really listening to me. You're not trying to one up me. You're trying to say, I heard you. I understood what we were saying below the baseball. And I'm going to show you that I was listening by reflecting theme, meaning a message back to you. So that's the kind of thing that I try to do all the time. And that comes through strategic listening that comes through not just listening by asking yourself while the person speaking, what are they really saying? What are they trying to express to me? What is the message beneath it? The best part of those moments is oftentimes people don't recognize what they're trying to say. They're sort of not storytellers. So they're saying something and they mean something, but they haven't really even analyzed what they mean. And so when you can tell them what they're saying without them even realizing what they were saying, that is profound. People connect instantaneously with you. It's almost like they think of you as like a magical therapist. Like, oh, my God, I didn't even realize I was feeling that way. But you're right. I do feel that way. That only comes through strategic listening, through saying to yourself, what is this person trying to express with the content that they are delivering at this moment? And when you can do that, I think magic happens. I think that's when people really want to connect with you. So as a very, very long answer to your question, Susan, which is always all my answers

## [27:19 - 30:22]

because there are always stories of some kind or another. I think adults don't like to hear about processes unless they've asked. Anyone who helped me reach this level, I'm going to make sure they get mentioned and I'm going to credit the hell out of them. That way, at least if I'm talking about something that I've positively done, I am on the backs of other people.
